title: Raducanu Reconnects with US Open-Winning Coach Ahead of Rome Return
summary:
Emma Raducanu's fairy-tale 2021 US Open run—winning the title as an 18-year-old qualifier without dropping a set—remains one of tennis's most astonishing feats. Now, as she gears up for a return to the tour at the Italian Open in Rome, she's briefly reunited with the coach who was in her box that day: Andrew Richardson.

Raducanu, now 23, spent a short training block at the Ferrer Academy near Benidorm, Spain, where Richardson serves as tennis director. The academy was founded by former world No. 3 David Ferrer, and Raducanu's advisers say she chose it to get valuable clay-court practice before the European swing. Her long history with Richardson, who also coached her as a junior, made it an appealing option.

However, there are currently no plans for the two to work together during the clay season or beyond. Raducanu has been without a full-time coach since parting ways with Francisco Roig after the Australian Open. She's been relying on hitting partner Alexis Canter for regular support and said in Indian Wells that she's "open" to hiring another coach but is wary of the intense scrutiny that comes with any decision.

Raducanu's last competitive match was in early March at Indian Wells; she missed the Miami Open due to a viral illness and skipped subsequent clay events to recover fully. In Rome, she'll practice with Germany's Eva Lys on Saturday and, as a seeded player, would receive a first-round bye, meaning she wouldn't play before Thursday.

The brief reunion with Richardson is a nostalgic nod to her breakthrough, but it also underscores the ongoing uncertainty in her coaching setup. For now, the focus is simply on getting match-fit and recapturing the form that once stunned the world.
why_it_matters: Raducanu’s coaching carousel and health struggles highlight the enormous mental and physical toll that early, unexpected success can place on young athletes. Her journey shows how behind every fairy-tale win are years of pressure, recovery setbacks, and the constant search for a stable support system — a challenge that resonates far beyond tennis.

keywords.explanation: A player who must win a series of pre-tournament matches just to earn a spot in the main draw, like a last-chance entry exam.
A dedicated period of practice, often at a specific facility, focused on improving skills and fitness before a series of competitions.
A tennis surface made of crushed brick or similar material that slows the ball and creates a high bounce, favoring defensive and consistent players.
Close, intense, and often critical observation or attention from the public, media, or experts.
A sickness caused by a virus — like a bad flu or cold — that can sideline an athlete for weeks and affect their fitness.
A privilege given to top-seeded players that allows them to skip the first round of a tournament and automatically advance to the next.

questions.question: What was Emma Raducanu's remarkable achievement at the 2021 US Open?
options: She reached the final but lost in straight sets.
She won the title as an 18-year-old qualifier without dropping a set.
She won a doubles title with a famous partner.
She was the first player from Great Britain to ever play the US Open.
questions.correct_answer: She won the title as an 18-year-old qualifier without dropping a set.

questions.question: Who is Andrew Richardson in relation to Raducanu?
options: Her current full-time coach who travels with her year-round.
A commentator who criticized her playing style.
The coach who was in her box when she won the US Open and whom she briefly reunited with for training.
A fitness trainer she hired only for clay-court tournaments.
questions.correct_answer: The coach who was in her box when she won the US Open and whom she briefly reunited with for training.

questions.question: Why did Raducanu train at the Ferrer Academy near Benidorm, Spain?
options: It is the official training base of the British Tennis Federation.
Andrew Richardson is the tennis director there, and she wanted clay-court practice before the European swing.
She was considering buying a vacation home in Spain.
The academy offers free coaching to all former Grand Slam champions.
questions.correct_answer: Andrew Richardson is the tennis director there, and she wanted clay-court practice before the European swing.

questions.question: What is Raducanu's current coaching situation?
options: She has a permanent contract with a new coach until 2026.
She relies on a hitting partner and has no full-time coach, having parted ways with Francisco Roig after the Australian Open.
She has been coaching herself without any support team.
She rehired Andrew Richardson as her full-time coach for the clay season.
questions.correct_answer: She relies on a hitting partner and has no full-time coach, having parted ways with Francisco Roig after the Australian Open.

questions.question: When did Raducanu last compete before the Italian Open in Rome?
options: January 2025 at the Australian Open.
Early March 2025 at Indian Wells, after which she missed events due to illness.
April 2025 at the Miami Open.
She has been playing nonstop since the US Open 2021.
questions.correct_answer: Early March 2025 at Indian Wells, after which she missed events due to illness.

questions.question: What advantage does Raducanu have as a seeded player in Rome?
options: She automatically qualifies for the semifinals.
She does not have to play any matches at all.
She receives a first-round bye and would not play before Thursday.
She gets to choose her first opponent from the unseeded players.
questions.correct_answer: She receives a first-round bye and would not play before Thursday.

background_read: Emma Raducanu's 2021 US Open win was historic: she became the first qualifier — male or female — to ever win a Grand Slam singles title, a feat that shot her from world No. 150 into the top 20 overnight.

Andrew Richardson is a British tennis coach who briefly worked with Raducanu after her junior career; he was replaced not long after her US Open triumph (a move that drew much criticism), and now runs the Ferrer Academy, founded by retired Spanish star David Ferrer (former world No. 3).

The clay-court season in Europe includes prestigious tournaments like the Italian Open in Rome, the Madrid Open, and Roland-Garros (the French Open), all played on a slow surface that tests stamina and point construction.

Coaching instability is common in tennis — top players often change coaches yearly or even mid-season — but for a young star like Raducanu, the constant scrutiny of each hire and firing can add immense mental pressure.

Raducanu has struggled with injuries and illnesses since her breakthrough, missing chunks of seasons; a 'first-round bye' as a seeded player means she gets extra days to prepare and recover before facing an opponent who already has a match under their belt.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Emma Raducanu has briefly reunited with Andrew Richardson, the coach from her fairy-tale 2021 US Open win, as she prepares for the Italian Open in Rome.
└─ SPECIFIC: The reunion is only for a short training block at the Ferrer Academy in Spain; there are no plans for a long-term partnership.
COACHING TIMELINE: Raducanu has been without a full-time coach since splitting with Francisco Roig after the Australian Open.
├─ CURRENT SUPPORT: She relies on hitting partner Alexis Canter and is 'open' to hiring a new coach but is wary of media scrutiny.
└─ STATED REASON FOR TRAINING: The academy was chosen to get valuable clay-court practice before the European swing.
HEALTH & RECOVERY: Her last match was in early March at Indian Wells; she missed the Miami Open and subsequent clay events due to a viral illness.
TOURNAMENT PLANS: In Rome, she will practice with Eva Lys and, as a seeded player, receives a first-round bye, meaning she wouldn't play before Thursday.
OPEN QUESTION: The brief, nostalgic reunion underscores the ongoing uncertainty in her coaching setup — the focus remains on getting match-fit and recapturing her stunning form.

perspectives.description: Training with a familiar face who was present during her greatest triumph could give Raducanu a confidence boost and a sense of comfort. The Ferrer Academy's clay-court expertise might help her regain form on a surface that could suit her game, and the seed in Rome gives her a less pressured path back to competition.

Yet another short-term coaching arrangement without a long-term plan raises questions about whether Raducanu is getting the consistent guidance she needs. The revolving door of coaches, combined with repeated illnesses and injuries, suggests a deeper struggle to build a sustainable career after that one magical run.

perspectives.perspective: Neutral / analytical (the nature of tennis success)
perspectives.description: Tennis history is full of players who made explosive breakthroughs only to face years of adjustment; Raducanu’s situation — balancing media scrutiny, sponsorship obligations, and a body not yet hardened to the tour grind — is a textbook case. Clay and grass each demand different skills, and building a team that can navigate all surfaces is uniquely challenging for a young, still-developing player.

perspectives.perspective: Forward-looking (road to recovery and long-term stability)
perspectives.description: If Raducanu can stay healthy and eventually settle on a long-term coaching partnership, she has the raw talent to contend again. The Rome tournament is a stepping stone, not an end goal; a steady clay-court season might rebuild her confidence, but the real test will be whether she can find a stable team to carry her through the next few years of her career.
